data.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again; Trump calls emergency Situation Room meeting
data.description:
With only three days left of the two-week ceasefire between the US and Iran, cracks are appearing on multiple fronts simultaneously.

On April 18, less than 24 hours after Iran announced the opening of the Strait of Hormuz, the Iranian military announced the reclosure of the strait. On the same day, Israel launched an attack in southern Lebanon, prompting Trump to convene an emergency White House Situation Room meeting.

According to media reports, a senior US official warned that if no breakthrough is achieved in the short term, the war could resume within days. Just the day before, Trump had stated that an agreement could be reached "within a day or two."

According to media reports, a senior US official warned that if no breakthrough is achieved in the short term, the war could resume within days. Just the day before, Trump had claimed that an agreement could be reached "within a day or two."

According to CCTV International News, the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy announced on the evening of the 18th local time that the Strait of Hormuz would be closed immediately until the US lifted its blockade of Iranian ships and ports due to the US violation of the ceasefire agreement. The Revolutionary Guard warned all ships in the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman not to leave their anchorages, and that any approach to the strait "would be considered cooperation with the enemy, and violating vessels would become targets." The statement also said, "The US president's remarks about the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf are completely unreliable." Just a day earlier, Iran had announced the opening of the strait to merchant ships sailing on designated routes. Trump immediately posted a "thank you" message, but then changed his tone, stating that "the maritime blockade against Iran remains in effect." Iran immediately accused the US of "maritime piracy." Iranian presidential spokesman Tabatabae stated on the 18th, "Due to the other side's repeated breaches of trust and its use of this significant concession for propaganda, the strait has been blocked again." After the strait was closed, chaos quickly ensued. According to media reports, the British Navy stated that an oil tanker was approached and fired upon by Revolutionary Guard speedboats near the coast of Oman, but the crew was safe; another container ship was hit by an unidentified projectile, damaging some containers. Several oil tankers attempting to pass through the strait turned back that day. However, some ships still attempted to seize the opening window from the previous day—a supertanker fully loaded with Qatari and Saudi crude oil, FPMC C Lord, transited the waters south of Iran's Larak Island towards the UAE, and several LNG carriers and oil tankers also headed towards the strait. Media analysis suggests that this reversal may expose the divide between hardliners in the Iranian military and the diplomatic system—the diplomacy's goodwill was quickly overturned by the military, reflecting the complexity and difficulty of the negotiations. Iran's newly appointed Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, stated in a declaration for National Army Day that day that "the navy is ready to make the enemy taste the bitter fruit of new defeats"; First Vice President Aref stated that Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz and will defend its rights "at the negotiating table or on the battlefield." The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most important energy transport routes. Before the outbreak of the US-Israel war against Iran, approximately one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas were transported through it. Trump convenes Situation Room meeting; US military prepares to board and seize Iranian ships. According to media reports, Trump convened an emergency meeting in the White House Situation Room on the morning of the 18th to discuss the Taiwan Strait crisis and negotiations with Iran. Vice President Vance, Secretary of State Rubio, Secretary of Defense Hergaces, and Treasury Secretary Bessenter were all present, along with White House Chief of Staff Wells, Special Envoy Witkov, CIA Director Ratcliffe, and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff Cain. Vance is expected to participate in the next round of negotiations with Iran. Military pressure is escalating simultaneously. According to Xinhua News Agency, citing the Wall Street Journal, the US military plans to board and seize oil tankers and merchant ships linked to Iran sailing in international waters in the coming days, expanding its reach beyond the Middle East. Xinhua also reported that Trump told reporters that the US is in talks with Iran, saying, "They want to block the Straits again, but Iran can't blackmail us. There will be news before the end of today." He had previously stated in an interview that Iran had agreed to an indefinite suspension of its nuclear program, and that "most of the key terms" had been finalized. However, he also threatened to resume bombing once the ceasefire expires. "Maybe I won't extend the ceasefire, so the blockade will continue. Unfortunately, we'll have to start throwing bombs again." The Lebanon-Israel ceasefire has been breached, and Hezbollah has issued a warning in response. The ceasefire agreement between Lebanon and Israel has already shown clear cracks after only one day in effect. According to CCTV News, the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) stated on the 18th that they had eliminated several Hezbollah members south of the Yellow Line in southern Lebanon within the past 24 hours, citing their violation of the ceasefire agreement and their "immediate threat." The IDF used artillery to support ground operations, destroying some Hezbollah infrastructure, and emphasized that the self-defense operation was "not bound by the ceasefire agreement." French President Macron confirmed on the same day that a French soldier was killed in an attack on UN peacekeeping forces in Lebanon, implying that Hezbollah should be held responsible. According to Xinhua News Agency, Hezbollah leader Qassem Soleimani issued a statement that day, saying he would respond to any violation of the ceasefire agreement, emphasizing that the ceasefire must be observed by both sides. The Lebanese military has previously recorded several attacks by the IDF, and some villages are still under intermittent shelling. The ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel, announced by Trump on April 16, will last for 10 days. There is a connection between the ceasefire and Iran's opening of the Strait of Hormuz—the simultaneous cracking of both lines has rapidly cooled previously escalating expectations for peace. The negotiation window is narrowing, and core differences remain unresolved. Despite the deteriorating situation, negotiations are still ongoing. According to Xinhua News Agency, Pakistani Army Chief of Staff Munir traveled to Tehran this week to mediate, and the US put forward new proposals. Iran's Supreme National Security Council stated that it is reviewing the proposals but has not yet responded. Media reports indicate that Trump has spoken with Munir and Iranian officials at least once. Media analysis suggests that the situation in the Taiwan Strait suddenly reversed precisely after the two sides narrowed their differences on uranium enrichment. Enriched uranium is a core bargaining chip in the negotiations. These materials were reportedly buried underground after the US and Israel bombed Iranian nuclear facilities during the 12-Day War last year, and their ownership is crucial to any agreement. Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagheri took a hard line: "Enriched uranium is as sacred to us as Iranian territory and will not be transferred anywhere under any circumstances." According to media reports, one of the proposed solutions is for the US to release $20 billion in frozen Iranian funds in exchange for Tehran handing over its enriched uranium stockpile, but Trump has repeatedly said "no" to this. The market has already priced in the expectation of peace. Brent crude oil plunged 9% to around $90 a barrel last Friday, erasing most of its gains since the outbreak of war; the spot benchmark dated Brent fell below $100 for the first time since March 11, while the stock market continued its rebound during the same period. But Bloomberg Economics analysts poured cold water on the idea in a report: "While an agreement seems within reach, it is unlikely to bring about comprehensive or lasting peace. We assess that any agreement will be limited and fragile."

data.title: Jensen Huang: Are top AI companies moving away from CUDA? "Your premise is wrong."
data.description:
For nearly two hours, Huang Renxun directly answered various questions about how Nvidia's market capitalization soared to $4 trillion during the era of big data.

Huang Renxun's new interview on "Silicon Valley's Most Popular Podcast" is packed with information.

Within half a day of its release, the video has already garnered over 100,000 views on YouTube alone.

Netizens also commented: It's rare to see Huang Renxun so excited.

TL;DR version, key points here:

  • Input is electronics, output is tokens, and in between is Nvidia.

  • AI will not make software cheap or homogenized; the widespread adoption of agents will rapidly increase tool deployment rates, leading to faster growth.

  • TPU poses no threat to Nvidia.

  • TPU poses no threat to Nvidia.

    • Nvidia GPUs make the invention of new algorithms easier. Nvidia's failure to invest in OpenAI and others earlier was a "misconception" and also "unavoidable." Nvidia's philosophy is "do as little as possible, but do what is necessary." Nvidia never prioritizes GPUs over funding. Even without deep learning, Nvidia will still focus on accelerating computing. More details are provided in the full transcript (including Huang's fierce rebuttal). Nvidia's competitive advantage (Question from podcast host Dwarkesh Patel, abbreviated as Q): Q: Software companies are experiencing a valuation crash because people believe AI will make software cheap. One potentially naive viewpoint is that Nvidia fundamentally makes software, while the manufacturing is done by others. If software becomes cheap, won't Nvidia lose its competitive advantage? Jensen Huang: Ultimately, there has to be something to convert electronics into tokens. This conversion from electronics to tokens, and the process of time making tokens more valuable, is very difficult to make homogeneous and cheap. The journey from electronics to tokens is incredible. The art, engineering, science, and invention invested in making one token more valuable than another are obvious. We are witnessing this process in real time, and everything involved is far from fully understood; this journey is far from over. I'm actually skeptical about whether your assumption will come true. Of course, we will make this process more efficient. Your question perfectly encapsulates Nvidia's operating philosophy: Input is electronics, output is tokens. In between lies Nvidia. Our job is to maximize this transformative capability by putting in the necessary effort while intervening as little as possible. "Intervening as little as possible" means delegating tasks not essential to us to our partners, making them part of the ecosystem. Today, Nvidia boasts the largest partner ecosystem, encompassing the entire supply chain, all computer companies, application developers, and model makers. Think of AI as a five-layer cake; our ecosystem covers every layer. We try to do as little as possible, but the part we have to do has proven incredibly challenging. I don't believe this part will become homogenized. In fact, I don't believe software companies or tool developers will lose their competitive advantage… most software companies today are tool developers. For example, Excel is a tool, PowerPoint is a tool, Cadence makes tools, and Synopsys also makes tools. My view is exactly the opposite; I believe the number of agents will grow exponentially, and the number of users of these tools will also grow exponentially. The deployment of these tools is likely to surge. Today, we are limited by the number of engineers. But in the future, there will be a large number of agents supporting engineers, exploring the design space in ways never seen before, and the tools we use today will not be discarded. I believe the proliferation of tools will lead to rapid growth for software companies. The reason it hasn't fully happened yet is because agents are not efficient enough in using these tools. Either these companies will build their own agents, or the agents will evolve to be able to use these tools efficiently. I think the two will combine. Q: In your recent filings, Nvidia's procurement commitments in areas such as foundries, memory, and packaging are close to $100 billion. SemiAnalysis says your related procurement commitments actually reach $250 billion. One explanation is that Nvidia's moat lies in securing the supply chain for these scarce components in advance. Is this Nvidia's biggest moat in the next few years? Jensen Huang: This is something we can do, but it's difficult for others. We've made huge commitments upstream. Some commitments are explicit, such as the procurement contracts you mentioned. Others are implicit, such as many upstream investments driven by our supply chain partners. I would say to the CEOs of these manufacturers, "Let me tell you how big this industry will be, let me explain why, let me work with you to project the future, and show you what I see." In this way, I constantly communicate with, motivate, and reach agreements with CEOs in different upstream industries. This makes them willing to invest. Why are they willing to invest in me, and not others? Because they know I have the ability to take over their supply and sell it through my downstream channels. The truth is, Nvidia's downstream supply chain and demand are enormous, and they're willing to invest in it. If you've attended GTC, you'll be amazed by its scale and the number of attendees. It's a complete 360-degree panorama, bringing together the entire universe of artificial intelligence. People come together because they need to understand each other. I bring them together so that downstream can see upstream, and upstream can see downstream, while also allowing everyone to see the latest developments in AI. Most importantly, they can also meet with AI-native companies and startups and witness firsthand everything I tell them. I spend a significant amount of time directly or indirectly communicating about future opportunities to our supply chain, partners, and ecosystem. People always say, "Jensen Huang, most of your keynote speeches are just one presentation after another." In fact, there's always a part of my presentations that's quite "torturous," almost like a lecture. I did it on purpose. I needed to make sure our entire supply chain—both upstream and downstream—understood the changes happening, why they were happening, when they were happening, and how large they were, and could reason about them as systematically as I could. Returning to the issue of the moat, we are preparing for the future—if our business reaches a trillion dollars in the next few years, our supply chain is ready for it. Without our market coverage and business drivers… just as cash flow has its liquidity, so does the supply chain. Without sufficiently frequent business liquidity, no one would be willing to build a supply chain to support architectural expansion. We can withstand such a large expansion because our downstream demand is enormous. And everyone has witnessed that. This allows us to accomplish everything we do at our current scale. Q: I'd like to understand more specifically whether the upstream is keeping up with demand. Over the past few years, your annual revenue has doubled, and the floating-point operations per second (flops) you provide globally has more than tripled.

    Jensen Huang: Doubling revenue at this scale is indeed incredible.

    Q: That's true. But when we talk about logic chips, you are TSMC's largest customer for the N3 node, and also one of the major customers for the N2 node. According to SemiAnalysis, AI will account for 60% of N3 capacity this year, and will reach 86% next year. In this situation, if you already occupy the majority of capacity, how do you continue to achieve double-digit growth? Is there a situation where the growth rate of AI computing power must slow down due to upstream factors? Have you seen a way to circumvent this problem? How do we double the capacity of the wafer fab every year?

    Jensen Huang: To some extent, instantaneous demand has exceeded the total supply of both upstream and downstream globally. At any given moment, we may be limited by the number of "plumbers," and that certainly happens. Q: So next year's GTC conference should invite plumbers to attend (laughs). Jensen Huang: That's a good idea (laughs). But having demand that exceeds industry supply capacity is a good thing. Obviously, the opposite is not good. If the gap between supply and demand is too large, the industry will quickly converge on the gap. For example, you'll find that almost no one talks about CoWoS packaging technology anymore. Q: Why? Jensen Huang: The reason is that the industry has invested heavily in this over the past two years, and the scale has even doubled several times. We are currently in a pretty good position in this regard. TSMC now knows that the supply capacity of CoWoS must keep up with the demand for logic chips and memory. They are expanding CoWoS and future packaging technologies to a level that is in sync with the development of logic chips. This is fantastic because for a time, CoWoS and HBM memory technologies were considered "specialty technologies." But now they've become mainstream computing technologies. Of course, now we can influence the supply chain much more broadly. I was saying a lot of what I'm saying now back in the early days of the AI ​​revolution. At that time, some people believed and invested, like Sanjay and his team at Micron. I was very impressed with that meeting; I clearly explained why things were happening this way and my predictions for the future. And they really doubled down, partnering with us in LPDDR and HBM memory. This undoubtedly brought tremendous growth to their companies. Some people arrived a little later, but they're all here now. We pay close attention to every bottleneck. Now we anticipate these bottlenecks years in advance. For example, our investments in collaborations with Lumentum, Coherent, and the silicon photonics ecosystem over the past few years have truly reshaped the supply chain. We've built a complete supply chain around TSMC, collaborated with them on the COUPE project, invented a bunch of new technologies, and licensed patents to the supply chain to keep it open. We help our partners expand capacity through new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, and investments. You can see that we're trying to ensure the supply chain can support this kind of large-scale development through ecosystem building. Q: It seems some bottlenecks are easier to solve than others. Scaling CoWoS to a larger scale might be relatively easy—Huang Renxun: By the way, I picked the most difficult example. Q: Which one? Huang Renxun: Plumbers and electricians. This is also one of the things that worries me about some "doomsday theorists" who always describe jobs being terminated and positions disappearing. If we advise people not to become software engineers, we will face a shortage of software engineers. Similarly, ten years ago, some pessimists made similar predictions, saying, "Whatever you do, don't become a radiologist." You can still find videos online saying that the profession of radiologist will be the first to disappear. But what has happened? We are precisely short of radiologists. Q: Returning to the earlier question about some bottlenecks that are easier to solve. How to manufacture twice the number of logic chips per year? The expansion of logic chips and memory chips is limited by extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV). How to achieve twice the growth per year? Jensen Huang: This can be scaled up quickly. These are not difficult; you just need to demand signals. Once you can make one, you can make ten, then a million. All of this is easily replicated. Q: How deeply will you get involved? Will you communicate with ASML and tell them, "Look at the demand three years from now. To achieve $2 trillion in revenue for Nvidia annually, we need more EUV lithography machines." Jensen Huang: Some I have to speak directly to, some are achieved indirectly. For example, if I convince TSMC, ASML will naturally be convinced. The key is that we must consider critical bottlenecks. But once TSMC is convinced, you will see enough EUV equipment within a few years. My view is that no bottleneck will last more than two or three years. At the same time, we are also making great progress in improving computing efficiency. For example, the efficiency improvement from Hopper to Blackwell architecture is 30-50 times. Because of the flexibility of CUDA, we are able to develop entirely new algorithms. In addition, we are increasing production capacity while improving computing efficiency. These issues are not so worrying to me. The real risks come from downstream issues, such as policies restricting energy expansion. You can't build an industry without energy; you can't build a new manufacturing company without energy. We want to reshape American industry. We want to bring back chip manufacturing, computer manufacturing, and packaging processes; we want to build new things, like electric cars and robots; we want to build AI factories. But you can't do all that without energy, and these problems will take a long time to solve. In contrast, the chip production capacity issue can be resolved in just 2-3 years. CoWoS capacity expansion will also take 2-3 years. Q: That's interesting. I feel like the guests I invite sometimes express completely opposite views. In those cases, I lack the technical knowledge to judge. Jensen Huang: The good news is you're talking to an expert right now (laughs). TPUs are not a threat; Nvidia is "redefining computing." Q: I have a question about competitors. Two of the world's top three AI models—Claude and Gemini—are trained on TPUs. What does this mean for Nvidia's future? Jensen Huang: What we're building is very different from TPUs. Nvidia is building Accelerated Computing, not just a Tensor Processing Unit (TPU). Accelerated Computing can be used for a wide range of purposes: molecular dynamics, quantum chromodynamics, data processing, data frames, structured and unstructured data. It's also used in fluid dynamics and particle physics. Furthermore, we use it for AI computing. Accelerated Computing is much more versatile. While everyone is talking about AI today, and AI is indeed very important and has a profound impact, the scope of computing is far broader than that. Nvidia has redefined computing, transitioning from general-purpose computing to accelerated computing. Our market reach is far greater than any TPU or ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) can achieve. We are the only company that can accelerate a wide range of applications. We have a huge ecosystem, so various frameworks and algorithms can run on Nvidia platforms. Furthermore, most self-built systems are not designed for easy operation by others. Our systems are ubiquitous, including on Google, Amazon, Azure, and OCI (Oracle Cloud Infrastructure), because anyone can use and operate our systems. If you want to operate this computing power through leasing, you'd better have a large, multi-industry customer ecosystem to absorb these resources. If you want to use it yourself, we can obviously help you operate these computing systems, for example, we support Elon Musk's xAI. Because we can support operators from any company and any industry, you can use it to build supercomputers specifically for scientific research and drug discovery, like the Eli Lilly. We can help them operate their own supercomputers to accelerate the entire diverse process of drug discovery and bioscience. There are a large number of applications that TPUs cannot cover. NVIDIA has built CUDA into an excellent tensor processing unit, but it can also handle the entire lifecycle of data processing, computing, AI, etc. Our market opportunity is broader and has a wider reach. Because we support all types of applications in the world, you can build an NVIDIA system anywhere and be sure there will be customer demand. This is a completely different concept. Q: Now for a long question. Your revenue is phenomenal, and this money doesn't come from pharmaceuticals or quantum computing. The reason you can have $60 billion in revenue per quarter is because AI is an unprecedented technology, and its growth rate is also unprecedented. So the question is, for artificial intelligence, what is the most suitable option? I'm not familiar with the details, but when I talked to my AI researcher friends, they said, "Look at the TPU, it's a large array of matrix multiplications, perfect for performing matrix multiplications, while GPUs are very flexible. GPUs excel when there are many branches or irregular memory accesses." But what is the essence of AI? It's just performing predictable matrix multiplications over and over again. You don't need to waste any chip area on warp schedulers or switching between threads and memory groups. And the TPU is indeed optimized for the main growth needs and use cases of AI computing today. I'd like to know your response to this. Jensen Huang: Matrix multiplication is indeed an important part of AI, but it's not everything. If you want to develop a new attention mechanism, decouple it in a different way, or invent a completely new architecture, such as a hybrid SSM (state-space model), you need a general-purpose programmable architecture. If you want to build a fusion of diffusion and autoregressive models, you also need a general-purpose programmable architecture. We can run everything you can imagine. This is our strength: **Our architecture makes it easy to invent new algorithms because it's a programmable system.** The ability to invent new algorithms is the real driving force behind the rapid advancements in AI. Devices like the TPU are also limited by Moore's Law, with a growth rate of approximately 25% per year. The only way to achieve a 10x or 100x leap is to fundamentally change the algorithms and the way we compute. This is NVIDIA's core strength. The reason we were able to achieve a 50x performance improvement from Hopper to Blackwell… when I first announced that Blackwell was 35x more energy efficient than Hopper, nobody believed me. Later, Dylan wrote an article pointing out that I was actually "deliberately conservative," and that it was actually 50x. This simply couldn't be achieved by relying solely on Moore's Law. Our approach to this problem is through new models, such as MoE, which parallelize, decouple, and distribute implementations within the computing system. Without CUDA support, developing such a new kernel would be virtually impossible. Our advantage lies in the programmability of NVIDIA's architecture, and also in our strong collaborative design capabilities. We can even offload some computation to the computing architecture, such as NVLink; or integrate it into the network, such as Spectrum-X. We can influence every aspect of the processor, system, architecture, library, and algorithm simultaneously. Without CUDA, I wouldn't even know where to begin developing. Q: This touches on an interesting question about the characteristics of NVIDIA's customer base. Currently, 60% of your revenue comes from the five major cloud service providers. In a different era, with different customers—for example, professors conducting experiments—they needed CUDA. They couldn't use other accelerators; they just needed to run PyTorch with CUDA and ensure everything was optimized smoothly. But these cloud service providers had the resources to write their own kernels. In fact, they had to do so to get that final 5% of performance needed for their specific architectures. Anthropic and Google have moved to their own accelerators, such as TPUs and Trainium. Even OpenAI, which uses NVIDIA GPUs, has developed tools like Triton because they need their own kernels. From CUDA C++ to cuBLAS and NCCL, they have a complete, independent stack that can be compiled to other accelerators. Given that most customers can and are actually building CUDA alternatives, is CUDA still the key reason why the cutting-edge AI field still chooses NVIDIA? Jensen Huang: CUDA is a rich ecosystem. If you want to develop software on any computer, choosing CUDA first is definitely a smart choice. Because the ecosystem is so rich, we support every development framework. If you want to create a custom kernel… for example, we have made huge contributions to Triton. Triton's backend incorporates a lot of NVIDIA technology. We are very happy to help each framework become perfect. There are many frameworks available, such as Triton, vLLM, SGLang, and many more emerging reinforcement learning frameworks like Verl and NeMo RL. The field of post-training and reinforcement learning is experiencing explosive growth. Therefore, if you're building on an architecture, CUDA is the wisest choice because you know the ecosystem is robust and reliable. You'll know that if something goes wrong, it's most likely in your code, not in the underlying massive amount of code. Don't forget, the amount of code you're dealing with when building these systems is enormous. When something doesn't work, is it you who's wrong, or is it the computer? You'll always hope it's you who's wrong and trust the computer's robustness. Of course, our own systems have problems, but they're deeply optimized, and you can at least build on that reliable foundation. That's the first point: the richness, programmability, and capabilities of the ecosystem. Secondly, if you're a developer, the most important thing when building anything is installing the foundation. You want your software to run on many other computers. You're not just building it for yourself, but for your team and even other teams. If you're a framework developer, NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem is an invaluable treasure trove of hardware and software. Hundreds of millions of NVIDIA GPUs are deployed worldwide, on every cloud platform. A10, A100, H100, H200, various L-series and P-series devices—a vast array of types and forms. We're practically everywhere. This massive installed base means that once your software or model is developed, it can run anywhere in the world—an invaluable asset. Finally, our widespread adoption on cloud platforms truly makes us unique. If you're an AI company or developer unsure which cloud service provider to work with or where to run your system, NVIDIA's system covers it all—including running directly within your company. The richness of this ecosystem, the breadth of its installed base, and the flexibility of its deployment models make CUDA irreplaceable. Q: That makes sense. I'm interested in whether these advantages are still as important to your major customers. For users in most industries, this may be very important. But for customers who can actually build their own software stacks—these customers make up the bulk of your revenue, especially in a world where AI is becoming increasingly powerful… the question ultimately becomes: if hyperscale enterprises can write their own kernels instead of relying on CUDA, can Nvidia maintain its current profit margins? Jensen Huang: The number of engineers we have deployed in these AI labs is staggering. We continuously optimize their software stacks because no one understands the complexity and details of their architecture better than we do. These architectures aren't as "general-purpose" as CPUs. A CPU is like a Cadillac—it runs smoothly, its performance doesn't fluctuate wildly, and anyone can drive it well. But NVIDIA's GPUs and accelerators are more like Formula One race cars. I can imagine anyone driving these GPUs at 100 mph, but to truly push them to their limits requires extremely high expertise. We also use a lot of AI to optimize our existing kernel libraries. I'm pretty sure our expertise will remain indispensable to our collaborating AI labs for a long time to come. We often enable them to further optimize their software stacks, resulting in 1 to 2x performance improvements. Sometimes optimizing a specific kernel can directly improve performance by 2x or 3x. This improvement is crucial for customers running large numbers of Hopper or Blackwell devices because it directly increases the efficiency of the entire facility, consequently boosting customer revenue. Without a doubt, NVIDIA's computing software stack offers the best total cost of ownership (TCO) in the world. No single platform offers a higher performance-to-TCO ratio than ours. The benchmarks are there, and I encourage TPUs or Trainium to use InferenceMAX and MLPerf to demonstrate their supposedly amazing inference cost advantages, but nobody's willing to do it. From a first-principles perspective, this simply doesn't make sense. I think the reason we're so successful is simple: our Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is outstanding. Secondly, you mentioned that 60% of our customers are from the top five cloud computing companies, but most of that business is actually directed towards external customers. They choose us because of our strong customer reach. We can bring them the best customers globally. These customers choose NVIDIA because of our unique breadth and versatility. I think the flywheel effect comes from several aspects: our installed base, the programmability of our architecture, the richness of our ecosystem, and the presence of a large number of AI companies. There are thousands of AI companies now. If you were one of these AI startups, which architecture would you choose? You would choose the most widely adopted architecture globally—that's us. You would also choose the architecture with the largest installed base—that's also us. And there's an architecture with a rich ecosystem—that's a unique advantage of NVIDIA. So, that's where the flywheel comes in. The core reasons for our success include: First, performance and cost advantages. Our performance per dollar is outstanding, resulting in the lowest cost for our customers. Second, energy efficiency advantages: our performance per watt is the highest globally. If a company builds a 1GW data center, that data center must be able to generate maximum revenue and as many tokens as possible, which directly translates into income. And we have the architecture with the most tokens per watt globally. Finally, if your goal is to rent out infrastructure, we have the most customers globally. Q: Interesting. I think the key issue is the market structure. Perhaps there exists a world with thousands of AI companies, each with roughly equal computing power. But from the perspective of the five major cloud service providers, those actually using these computing resources are Anthropic, OpenAI, and large foundational model labs capable of building their own accelerators.

    Huang Renxun: No, I think your assumption is wrong.

    Q: Maybe, but let me ask you a slightly different question.

    Huang Renxun: No, let me correct your assumption.

    Q: Okay. Let me ask you another question.

    Huang Renxun: But still, please make sure I correct this assumption. Because this is too important for AI, too important for the future of science, and too important for the future of the industry. This assumption… let me explain— Q: Let me finish the question first, then we can discuss this topic together. Jensen Huang: Okay. Q: If the metrics regarding price, performance, and performance per watt are true, what's your take on this? For example, Anthropic recently announced a multi-gigawatt TPU computing agreement with Broadcom and Google, where most of their computations are done via TPUs. Clearly, for Google, TPUs provide the primary computing resources. And from my observation, these large AI companies seem to have relied heavily on Nvidia for most of their computing resources… but not anymore. So, if these parameters are accurate on paper, what's your take on these companies still choosing other accelerators? Jensen Huang: Anthropic is a special case, not a trend. Would TPUs have grown without Anthropic? They're entirely driven by Anthropic. Would Trainium have grown without Anthropic? Also entirely driven by Anthropic. This isn't about a large number of ASIC opportunities, but rather the existence of only one Anthropic. Q: But the collaboration between OpenAI and AMD… they're developing their own Titan accelerator. Jensen Huang: Yes, but we can all acknowledge that OpenAI's main computing still relies on Nvidia. We're still collaborating extensively. I don't mind other companies trying different things. How will they know how good we are if they don't try these products? We also need to be reminded that we must continue to work hard to maintain our current position. There will always be exaggerated claims. But look at the number of ASIC projects that have been cancelled in the past. Making a product better than Nvidia isn't easy. It's not wise either. Of course, Nvidia will definitely have its shortcomings, but in terms of scale and speed, we are the only company that is driving significant technological leaps every single year—every single year. Q: I think their logic might be: "Hey, these products don't need to be better, as long as they're not 70% worse than Nvidia's," because buying from you means paying 70% profit. Jensen Huang: Don't forget, even ASICs have very high profit margins. Assuming Nvidia's profit margin is 70%, ASIC's profit margin is close to 65%. How much do you actually save? Q: You mean Broadcom? Jensen Huang: Yes. You always have to pay a company. From the data I'm familiar with, ASIC profit margins are extremely high. They themselves think so, and they're proud of their astonishing ASIC profit margins. A long time ago, we didn't have the capacity to do this. Back then, I didn't fully realize how difficult it is to build a foundational AI lab like OpenAI or Anthropic; they require huge investments from vendors. We couldn't provide billions of dollars in investment to get Anthropic to use our computing resources, but Google and AWS could. They invested heavily early on, eventually getting Anthropic to use their computing resources. We couldn't do that back then. My mistake was not recognizing that AI labs have no choice; venture capital firms will never invest $5 billion to $10 billion in a lab. But even if I had understood that, I don't think we could have done it back then. Fortunately, I won't make the same mistake again. I'm happy to invest in OpenAI and help them scale. I'm also glad that when Anthropic approached us, we were able to invest in and support them. We couldn't do it in the past, and if I could do it again—if Nvidia had the scale we have today—I would be more than happy to do it. Why doesn't Nvidia become a super cloud service provider? Q: That's very interesting. For years, Nvidia has been the most profitable company in the AI ​​field. Now you're investing; reportedly, you've invested as much as $30 billion in OpenAI and $10 billion in Anthropic. And now, their valuations have grown significantly, and I believe they will continue to grow. So, over the years, you've been providing computing power to these companies, and you can see where they're headed. A few years ago, even just a year ago, their valuations were only a tenth of what they are now, and you had plenty of cash on hand then. Logically, one possibility is that Nvidia could have built its own basic research lab and made huge investments to make all of this possible, or completed the deal you're doing now much earlier, before the high valuation. I'm curious, why didn't you do it sooner?

    Huang Renxun: We did it as soon as we could. If the conditions had been available earlier, I would have done it even earlier. But when Anthropic needed us to do it, we didn't have the conditions. It wasn't a reasonable choice for us at the time.

    Q: Why? Was it because of funding?

    Huang Renxun: Yes, it was about the scale of the investment. At that time, we had never invested in external companies, especially not on such a large scale. We didn't realize it was necessary. I always thought they could go to venture capital like other companies. But the goals they wanted to achieve couldn't be achieved through venture capital. OpenAI's goals couldn't be achieved through venture capital either. I realize that now, but I didn't understand it then. But that's also their cleverness. They realized early on that they had to do this. I'm glad they made that choice back then. Although it forced Anthropic to find someone else, I'm still happy for them to exist. Anthropic's existence is a good thing for the world, and I'm genuinely happy about it. Q: Of course, you're still making a lot of money, and more every quarter. Jensen Huang: Even so, there can still be regrets. Q: The question remains—now that you have a lot of money on hand and keep making more, what should you do with that money? One answer is that an ecosystem of intermediaries is emerging, enabling these research labs to convert financial expenditures into operational expenses so that these labs can rent computing resources. Chips are very expensive, but they generate tremendous value over their lifecycle as AI models become increasingly powerful. Nvidia has ample funds to cover such capital expenditures. In fact, it's reported that you've provided up to $6.3 billion in support for CoreWeave and invested $2 billion in it. So why doesn't Nvidia become a cloud service provider itself and lease these computing resources? Jensen Huang: This is a matter of company philosophy. Nvidia should do "what must be done, but as little as possible." This means that the work we're doing to build computing platforms is this: if we don't do it, I genuinely believe no one else will. If we didn't build NVLink the way we do it now, if we didn't build the entire technology stack the way we do it now, if we didn't build the entire ecosystem the way we do it now, if we didn't persist in building CUDA for the past 20 years—most of that time was unprofitable—if we didn't do all of this, no one else will. If we hadn't created all the CUDA-X libraries, making them domain-specific… Over a decade ago, we started focusing on domain-specific libraries. We realized that if we hadn't created these libraries, whether for ray tracing, image generation, or early AI development, then technologies like data processing, structured data processing, and vector data processing wouldn't exist. We even created a library called cuLitho for computational lithography. If we hadn't created it, no one else would have. So, if we hadn't done this work, accelerated computing wouldn't be where it is today. So, this is something we absolutely must do. We should go all out and do everything we can to accomplish it. However, there are many cloud service providers in the world; if we don't do it, someone else will. NVIDIA's philosophy is to do "what must be done, but less is more," and everything revolves around this core principle. Regarding cloud services, if we didn't support the existence of "new cloud service providers" like CoreWeave, then these AI cloud companies wouldn't exist. Without our support, CoreWeave wouldn't exist. Without our support, Nscale wouldn't be where it is today. Without our support, Nebius wouldn't be where it is today. And now, they're thriving.

    Q: Why don't you pick the winners?

    Huang Renxun: First, it's not our responsibility. Second, when Nvidia was founded, there were 60 companies doing 3D graphics. Finally, only we survived. But if you had asked which of those 60 companies would have survived, Nvidia would likely have been listed as the least likely.

    Back then, Nvidia's graphics architecture was completely wrong. Not a little wrong, but completely wrong.

    We designed an architecture that developers simply couldn't support. It could never succeed. We reasoned based on correct first principles, but ultimately arrived at the wrong solution.

    Back then, everyone would have excluded us from the competition. But look at where we are now. Therefore, I know I need to be humble enough. Don't pick winners. Either let them compete among themselves, or support everyone. Q: There's something I don't understand. You said Nvidia doesn't prioritize supporting new cloud service companies, but then listed many new cloud service companies and said, "They wouldn't exist without Nvidia's support." How are these two statements compatible? Jensen Huang: First, they need to have the will to survive and actively seek our help. When they desire to exist and have their own business plans, expertise, and passion—obviously they must possess some capabilities. But ultimately, they need some investment to establish themselves, and we will support them. The sooner we get their flywheel effect going, the better. Your question is, "Do we want to be financial investors?" The answer is no. Fundraising is someone else's domain; we prefer to partner with everyone involved in fundraising rather than become financiers ourselves. Our goal is to focus on what we do best, keeping our business model as simple as possible while supporting our ecosystem. For example, when OpenAI needed up to $30 billion in investment, we stepped in to help them. The world needs them. The world craves their presence, and I want them to exist. They have strong growth momentum right now. We will support them and help them scale. We will make this investment because they need us. But we're not trying to do "as much as possible," but "as little as possible." Q: This question might seem obvious, but we've been facing a GPU shortage for years, and now, with advancements in models, the supply-demand gap seems even larger. Jensen Huang: That's right, GPUs are still in short supply. Q: Yes. Nvidia allocates scarce resources in a unique way, not simply on a highest bidder basis, but more on the premise that "we want these new cloud service companies to exist," thus allocating some resources to companies like CoreWeave, Crusoe, and Lambda. Why does Nvidia take this approach? Do you agree with this market description? Jensen Huang: No, no, your premise is wrong. We are very cautious about these things. First, if you don't place an order, then no amount of discussion will help. We can't do anything until we receive an order. So the first step is: we work with everyone to do demand forecasting, because these things take a long time to produce, and data center construction also takes a long time. We coordinate supply and demand through forecasting, that's the first step. Secondly, we do demand forecasting with as many people as possible, but ultimately it comes down to actual orders. What can we do if, for some reason, you don't place an order? After a certain point, it's on a "first-come, first-served" basis. However, if your data center isn't ready, or some components aren't available to get it up and running, we might prioritize other customers. This is simply to maximize the utilization of our own factory capacity; we might make some of these adjustments. Otherwise, the priority is "first-come, first-served." You need to place an order. If you don't, there's really nothing you can do. Of course, this could turn into some stories, like the previous reports about Larry Page, Elon Musk, and I having dinner together, asking for GPUs. That's completely untrue. We did have dinner together, and it was a very pleasant dinner. But they absolutely didn't beg for GPUs. They just placed an order. Once the order is placed, we do our best to meet their needs. It's not complicated. Q: Okay, so it sounds like there's a queue. If your data center is ready and the order is placed at a certain time, then you get delivered in order. But it still doesn't sound like the highest bidder gets priority. Why take this strategy? Huang: We never do that. Q: Okay. Huang: We never do. Q: Why not sell to the highest bidder? Huang: Because that's bad business practice. You set a price and then let people decide whether to buy. I know other companies in the chip industry adjust prices when demand is high, but we don't. We've never done that. You can rely on us. I'd rather be the foundation of the industry, without customers having to guess. If we give you a quote, that's the final price. If demand skyrockets, let it skyrocket. Q: On the other hand, this is also why you have such a good working relationship with TSMC, right? Jensen Huang: Yes, Nvidia and TSMC are about to celebrate their 30th anniversary. We haven't even signed a legal contract. Some things are generally about fairness; sometimes I benefit, sometimes I suffer. But overall, we have an excellent relationship. I can completely trust them and rely on them completely. What you can trust about Nvidia is this: every year, you can expect progress from us. This year it's Vera Rubin, next year it's Vera Rubin Ultra, then Feynman, and the year after that might be a new product yet to be named. Every year, we give you something to look forward to. Looking across the entire ASIC field, it's hard to find another team so consistently reducing per-chip costs by an order of magnitude every year while maintaining high productivity.

    Even without deep learning, Nvidia will still do accelerated computing

    Q: An interesting question. Suppose you already occupy most of TSMC's 3nm process capacity, and will continue to do so at the 2nm node. Do you think that, given the enormous demand for AI and the inability of cutting-edge capacity to meet it, you could go back and utilize the remaining capacity of older process nodes like 7nm, for example, to manufacture a chip based on the Hopper or Ampere architecture, but combined with existing numerical optimization techniques and the other improvements you mentioned? Do you think we will see this situation before 2030?

    Huang Renxun: There's no need for that. The reason is that each generation of architecture relies on more than just transistor process technology. Engineering design, packaging, stacking, numerical optimization, and various improvements in system architecture all play a significant role. If we encounter insufficient production capacity, we have to revert to older process nodes and redesign the chip... The R&D investment required is something no one can afford. We can afford the investment to move forward, but we can't afford the cost of going back. Of course, if the situation is... let's do a thought experiment: if one day we conclude, "We can no longer obtain more cutting-edge production capacity," if that day really comes, I will certainly immediately choose to go back to using the 7nm process. Q: Someone raised a question: why doesn't Nvidia simultaneously develop multiple chip projects using different architectures? For example, you can develop wafer-level chips like Cerebras, or large packages like Dojo, or even a design without CUDA at all. You have enough resources and engineering talent to carry out these projects in parallel. So why put all your bets in one basket? Jensen Huang: Oh, we can do that. But the problem is, we haven't found a better idea. We could try these things, but they weren't any better. We tested all of them in the simulator, and the conclusion was clear: they were worse. So we won't do them. The project we're currently focusing on is exactly what we want to do. Of course, if the task categories change significantly—I'm not referring to algorithms, but rather a real change in task requirements, depending on the market landscape—then we might decide to add some other accelerators. For example, we recently introduced Groq and integrated it into the CUDA ecosystem. We did this because the value of tokens is incredibly high now, and this allows us to set different prices for them. A few years ago, tokens were either free or inexpensive. But now, our customers are more diverse, and they need different performance levels. For example, our software engineers, if I can provide a token with faster response times, making them more efficient than they are now, I'm willing to pay for it. This market is relatively recent. I think we can now segment the market based on response time. This is why we decided to expand the Pareto frontier and create an inference segment with faster response times, despite its lower throughput. Previously, increasing throughput had been a greater priority. But we believe there might be a future scenario where even with lower throughput, it makes sense due to a higher average selling price (ASP). That's why we did it. But overall, from an architectural perspective, if I had more resources, I would invest them in Nvidia's existing architecture. Q: I find this idea of ​​"high-premium tokens" and inference market segmentation very interesting. Jensen Huang: Yes, further market segmentation. Q: Okay, one last question. What would Nvidia be doing now if the deep learning revolution had never happened? Jensen Huang: Accelerating Computing – What We've Always Been Doing. We believe Moore's Law is slowing down… General-purpose computing performs well in many areas, but not so well in many computational tasks. Therefore, we combine an architecture called GPU with the CPU to accelerate the CPU's computational load. Different code kernels or algorithms can be offloaded to run on our GPUs. The result is that you can speed up an application by 100x, 200x. Where can this performance be used? Obviously in engineering and science fields, such as physics, data processing, computer graphics, image generation, and so on. Even without AI today, Nvidia would still be a very large company. There's a very fundamental reason for this: the potential for further expansion of general-purpose computing power has essentially reached its limit. A more feasible approach is domain-specific accelerators. One of our earliest ventures was computer graphics, but there are many others, such as particle physics and fluid simulations, structured data processing, and various algorithms that benefit from CUDA technology. Our mission has always been to bring accelerated computing to the world, driving applications that general-purpose computing couldn't achieve, and helping to push the boundaries of science. Some early applications included molecular dynamics, seismic processing for energy exploration, image processing, and all aspects of computer graphics—areas where general-purpose computing was far too inefficient. Without AI, I would be deeply disappointed. But it is because of our advancements in computing technology that deep learning has become ubiquitous. We enable researchers, scientists, and students to conduct amazing scientific research with a PC or a GeForce graphics card. This commitment has never changed, not even a little bit. If you watch GTC, the opening section has nothing to do with AI. Computational lithography, quantum chemistry research, data processing—these topics are unrelated to AI, but they are still very important. I know AI is exciting, but many people are doing important work that doesn't involve AI, and these computational tasks are not limited to tensor computation.

data.title: The biggest DeFi heist of 2026 – hackers steal from Aave and then conveniently scam it as well.
data.description:
Author: Xiaobing, Deep Tide TechFlow

At 17:35 (UTC) on April 18th, a wallet laundered with Tornado Cash sent a cross-chain message to LayerZero's EndpointV2 contract.

The message's semantics were simple: a user on a certain chain wanted to transfer rsETH back to the Ethereum mainnet. LayerZero faithfully relayed the instruction according to the protocol design. The bridge contract deployed by Kelp DAO on the mainnet also faithfully executed the release as designed.

116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million at the time, were transferred to an address controlled by the attacker in a single transaction.

The problem is, no one on the other chain had ever stored this rsETH. This "cross-chain request" was fabricated out of thin air; LayerZero believed it, and the Kelp bridge also believed it.

46 minutes later, Kelp's emergency multisignature was finally paused. By then, the attackers had completed the second half of their operation, staking the stolen, essentially unpegged rsETH into Aave V3 and borrowing approximately $236 million worth of wETH.

This is the largest DeFi theft so far in 2026, exceeding the Drift protocol's loss by several million dollars more than the North Korean-backed hack on April 1st. But what truly sends chills down the spines of the industry is not just the amount.

How the attack happened: Three bets between 17:35 and 18:28

Let's reconstruct the timeline.

17:35 UTC, the first successful bet.

The attacker invoked the `lzReceive` function on the LayerZero EndpointV2 contract, a wallet backed by Tornado Cash, to send a forged cross-chain data packet to Kelp's bridging contract. Contract verification passed, and 116,500 rsETH were released to the attacker's address. A single transaction. Clean. At 18:21 UTC, Kelp's emergency suspension of multisignature froze the rsETH core contract on the mainnet and multiple L2 servers. 46 minutes after the attack. At 18:26 and 18:28 UTC, the attacker launched two more attempts, each with a LayerZero data packet attempting to withdraw another 40,000 rsETH (approximately $100 million). Both attempts were reverted, and the contract was frozen, but the attacker was clearly still trying to abscond with the remaining liquidity. Nearly three hours passed between the initial success and Kelp's public statement. Kelp's first X post wasn't published until 8:10 PM UTC, and its wording was very restrained: Suspicious cross-chain activity involving rsETH has been discovered; the mainnet and multiple rsETH contracts on L2 have been suspended; and they are collaborating with LayerZero, Unichain, auditors, and external security experts on root cause analysis. However, ZachXBT, a blockchain detective, gave a conclusion even earlier than the official statement. He issued an alert on his Telegram channel before 3 PM ET, listing six wallet addresses related to the theft and pointing out that the attacking wallets had all prepared funds through Tornado Cash before the operation began. He didn't name the Kelp DAO, but on-chain analysts connected the addresses in just a few hours. This was a premeditated operation executed within minutes. The pre-charged, laundered wallets, the meticulously crafted cross-chain data packets, and the coordinated attack and Aave collateralized lending—every step was executed with perfect timing. After stealing, they also planned to scam. If it were just a simple bridging vulnerability, stealing 116,500 rsETH and running away, it would at most be considered a major incident in 2026. Kelp would absorb the loss, the community would digest it for a few days, and the industry would move forward. But the attackers clearly did the math. rsETH itself doesn't have ample secondary liquidity. Directly dumping $292 million into DEXs for a sell-off would result in slippage eating up a significant portion of the profits. A more elegant way to exit was to package this "newly acquired rsETH" as seemingly respectable collateral and borrow truly liquid assets through lending protocols. The attackers then took a second step: depositing the stolen rsETH into Aave V3 as collateral to borrow a large amount of wETH. Why was this step fatal? Because at that moment, the Aave contract was still calculating the collateral value based on the oracle price of rsETH, while the reserves in the bridge had been emptied. The underlying economic entity of this batch of rsETH no longer existed. The lending protocol was still issuing loans according to the "100% gold content" standard, but the collateral was now a worthless check. The result was that the attackers transferred the risk of liquidating their funds to Aave's wETH reserve pool. Aave V3's wETH reserves are currently absorbing bad debts. Solidity developers and auditor 0xQuit reminded depositors on X that the wETH pool has actually been damaged, and some withdrawals may only be restored after Aave's Umbrella backup module clears the deficit. The latest estimate for bad debt is in the range of $177 million, and this is only on the Ethereum mainnet side. A prophetic first major test. For veteran DeFi players, this feels familiar; Aave V2's Safety Module played a similar role during the Luna crash in 2022. But this time, it's Umbrella. Aave will launch a new generation backup system in late 2025 to replace the old Safety Module, and this event is the first major real-world stress test of Umbrella's automatic bad debt coverage mechanism. Umbrella's logic is straightforward: stake aWETH, aUSDC, GHO, and other aTokens into the corresponding Umbrella vault to earn extra incentives. However, when the corresponding asset pool incurs a deficit, this staked portion will be slashed proportionally to cover the shortfall. This design looks good on paper. In the first month of Aave v3.3's operation, the total pool deficit was approximately $400, corresponding to nearly $9.5 billion in outstanding loans—a negligible proportion. However, the $177 million in bad debt is on a completely different scale. For users who staked aWETH in Umbrella, this means they will soon truly feel the weight of "bearing the risk of slashing" for the first time. Aave's official statement is cautious: if bad debt occurs, Aave plans to use Umbrella assets to cover any financial shortfall. However, whether it can be fully covered, what the slashing ratio is, and how much principal stakers have lost—these questions will only be answered after the settlement process is complete. The Original Sin of Cross-Chain Bridges Even more disturbing is the identity of the stolen rsETH. rsETH is deployed on more than 20 networks, including Base, Arbitrum, Linea, Blast, Mantle, and Scroll, with cross-chain transactions handled by LayerZero's OFT standard. The rsETH in the emptied bridges is the reserve that supports all the "wrapped" versions of rsETH on these networks. This design sounds conventional at first glance: the mainnet vault holds a 1:1 reserve, and rsETH holders on L2 can theoretically cross back to the mainnet to redeem their rsETH at any time. But this mechanism relies on the premise that the vault actually has money. The vault is now 18% empty. Approximately 18% of Kelp's total rsETH circulating supply lost its corresponding reserves overnight. This creates a feedback loop: panic redemptions by L2 holders will transmit pressure to the unaffected Ethereum supply, potentially forcing Kelp to unstake its positions to meet withdrawal requests. Restaking isn't a simple matter of pressing a button. EigenLayer withdrawals have a delay, and underlying validators have queuing periods for exits. If L2 rsETH holders rush to the redemption window, Kelp may not have enough time to prepare the mainnet's repayment ammunition. This is a fundamental risk of the bridge reserve model: if the mainnet, this single reservoir, fails, the pressure in all downstream channels will collapse. Every rsETH holder on every L2 blockchain is now facing the same choice: run first, or trust Kelp to bail them out? Panic swept across the entire DeFi lending sector within hours. Aave V3 and V4's rsETH market was frozen, and new deposits and rsETH-based lending channels were shut down. SparkLend and Fluid followed suit, freezing the rsETH market. Ethena, while stating it had no rsETH exposure and maintained over 101% overcollateralization, still suspended its LayerZero OFT bridge from the Ethereum mainnet as a precaution, with an expected suspension of about six hours. This reaction is quite intriguing: even players without direct exposure are suspending LayerZero-related bridges. Lido Finance has suspended new deposits for its earnETH product (because the product contains rsETH exposure), while emphasizing that stETH and wstETH are unaffected and that the Lido core staking protocol is unrelated to this event. Upshift has suspended deposits and withdrawals for High Growth ETH and Kelp Gain vaults. This list is still growing. Deep Tide Commentary: The Road to DeFi Security is Long and Arduous. As of this writing, the root cause analysis of Kelp DAO is still ongoing. How much of the stolen rsETH can be recovered through negotiations with security teams or white-hat hackers? Can Aave's Umbrella withstand this bad debt? Will rsETH holders on L2 trigger a run? Can the prices of AAVE and rsETH stabilize before the end of the weekend? However, some problems have already emerged. For example, can LRT continue to be a suitable collateral for lending protocols? Liquid Restaking Tokens were the darlings of the Ethereum ecosystem in the last cycle. EigenLayer initiated the narrative of "earning multiple layers of returns with one ETH," and protocols like Kelp, ether.fi, and Puffer industrialized this narrative. The end result was that LRT was included in the collateral whitelist by major lending protocols as a structured asset. This decision was based on the assumption that LRT's anchoring mechanism was robust enough, and that the risks of multi-layered nesting of underlying assets could be fully modeled and isolated at the smart contract level. The Kelp incident, in just one afternoon, exposed a major flaw in this assumption. LRT's risks stem not only from its underlying smart contracts but also from its cross-chain distribution architecture; not only from a single protocol but also from every dependency it has with EigenLayer, LayerZero, and Aave. Each individual piece of the DeFi Lego set may appear safe individually, but when they are assembled, the risks are multiplicative, not additive. In the coming months, all lending protocols still using LRT as high-grade collateral will need to reassess their risk parameters. Supply caps will decrease, liquidation buffers will increase, and some protocols may be delisted. DeFi's moat has always been called "composability," but this incident reminds everyone that composability is a double-edged sword. The network effects you pride yourself on are amplifiers in the hands of attackers. This attacker had planned their exit strategy beforehand. They weren't just stealing; they were using DeFi's composability as a weapon. The tighter the dependencies between protocols and the richer the composability, the wider the attacker's attack surface, and the more financial tools they can utilize. DeFi security remains a long and arduous task.

data.title: Payward raises $200 million from 17 projects that have raised $443 million.
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

This week, a total of 17 blockchain startups completed funding rounds, raising a total of $443 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Payward raised $200 million from Deutsche Börse Group.

Payward is the parent company of Kraken and its related products, and its business covers crypto trading, custody, payments, lending, on-chain finance, and benchmark services. Its positioning is to provide infrastructure for an open, programmable global market, connecting crypto assets, tokenized assets, and traditional finance through a unified, interoperable technology stack.

The company states that its business covers 190+ jurisdictions and holds 100+ licenses and registrations, encompassing crypto, securities, derivatives, payments, remittances, banking, and lending. Claw Intelligence has completed a $3 million seed funding round, with investors including Castrum Capital, Titans Ventures, Super Labs, and Genesis Capital VC. Claw Intelligence is an AI-powered Web3 intelligence and trading platform focused on the BNB Chain. It integrates market data, on-chain signals, research analysis, and execution tools into a single interface, aiming to transform fragmented blockchain information into structured, actionable insights. Its chat-based and modular design allows users to more efficiently transition from analysis to trade execution. KieDex Raises $3.5 Million Seed Funding. KieDex is a crypto futures trading platform focused on the high-leverage perpetual contract market, employing a "trade-to-earn" model. Positioned as a fast and simple exchange, users can start trading with free USDT and earn KDX rewards through trading activities. The platform emphasizes speed, fairness, and long-term incentives, leaning more towards derivatives DeFi trading than general-purpose exchanges or infrastructure protocols. Mirage Raises Seed Funding (Amount Undisclosed). Mirage is a non-custodial privacy protocol for stablecoin transfers on Ethereum and other EVM-compatible networks. It enables privacy payments similar to regular on-chain transactions without pooling funds. Mirage focuses on fast settlement, compliant integration, and easy embedding into wallets, payment applications, custody services, and DeFi products. Its core goal is to achieve low-friction, high-availability, and broadly compatible "privacy digital dollar transfers." Nava Labs raised $8.3 million in seed funding. Nava is a middleware verification layer for AI Agent blockchain transactions. Before funds are transferred, it verifies that the Agent's on-chain execution conforms to its original instructions. Through arbitration verification, execution custody, and a dedicated coordination layer (Nava Chain), the system performs security, correctness, and policy compliance analysis on transactions. Its goal is to make AI-automated DeFi operations more reliable, auditable, and financially secure, making it more suitable for institutional scenarios. Pumpcade raised $5 million in seed funding. Pumpcade is a Solana-based platform for instantly creating and trading meme tokens. It simplifies the token issuance process, allowing users to quickly publish and list assets without complex technical skills. The platform is community-driven, promoting participation, discovery, and viral spread within the meme token ecosystem. SHARE has completed its seed round of funding (amount undisclosed). Share is a decentralized internet infrastructure network (DePIN) focused on solving connectivity issues in Africa and emerging markets. Through a distributed node network, it reduces fragmentation, high costs, and dependence on centralized service providers. The platform provides open, reliable, and low-cost internet access and data services. Its goal is to improve accessibility and drive the growth of the digital economy in underdeveloped regions. Other Funding Rounds Mindra AI completed a strategic funding round (amount undisclosed). Mindra AI is a decentralized Large Model (LLM) infrastructure network that builds a "Intelligence Grid." It supports dynamic inference routing, cross-chain agent collaboration, and introduces AI-Fi to financialize computing resources. Through DeAI technology, it enhances network intelligence, scalability, and user experience while reducing reliance on centralized AI providers. Spektr completed a $20 million Series A funding round. Spektr is an AI-driven compliance operations platform that helps banks, fintech, and Web3 companies automate KYC, KYB, customer onboarding, monitoring, remediation, document review, ownership structure analysis, and risk assessment. It combines configurable processes with AI agents, enabling teams to scale compliance capabilities and reduce manual costs. Slash has completed a $100 million Series C funding round. Slash is a vertically integrated digital banking platform built specifically for online businesses. It offers checking accounts, virtual/physical cards (with cashback), transfer services, and integration with mainstream accounting software. It supports multi-user, real-time account partitioning, and seamless management of crypto and fiat currencies, serving e-commerce, marketing, and crypto-native enterprises. ZenGo was acquired by eToro for $70 million. ZenGo is a self-custodied crypto wallet that uses MPC technology to eliminate mnemonic phrase risk. It supports 380+ assets and provides 3D facial recognition and triple authentication recovery mechanisms. Brix raised $5.5 million. Brix is ​​a DeFi platform that brings institutional-grade strategies on-chain through structured yield products. Its first asset, iTRY, is a token based on the Turkish Lira Money Market Fund, allowing users to participate in arbitrage trading that was previously limited to banks. BlockInvest raised $4.7 million. BlockInvest is a Web3 investment and asset management platform for financial institutions. It leverages blockchain and AI to tokenize assets and improve data quality and liquidity. Totalis raised $500,000. Totalis is an on-chain prediction market derivatives layer that supports the construction of parlays. It covers multiple sectors including geopolitics, crypto, and sports. Paxos Labs (Amplify) raised $12 million. Paxos Labs is an enterprise-grade on-chain financial platform incubated by Paxos. Its core product, Amplify, provides a unified SDK that supports the integration of yield, lending, and stablecoin issuance functions. OpenGradient raised $9.5 million in funding. This "hybrid execution" supports seamless interoperability between smart contracts in different languages ​​(Solidity, Rust, etc.).
